title: Carer's Leave Act 2023
chapters: 3 sections + Schedule (inserting new ss.80J–80N into the Employment Rights Act 1996), supplemented by the Carer's Leave Regulations 2024 (SI 2024/468)

parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Wendy Chamberlain MP (Liberal Democrat, North East Fife) — Private Members' Bill via ballot procedure
commonsSponsors: Wendy Chamberlain MP (Liberal Democrat, North East Fife)
otherParliamentaryChampions: Baroness Pitkeathley (former Chief Executive, Carers UK)
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: s.3 (extent, commencement, short title) commenced on Royal Assent. s.1 and Schedule commenced 4 December 2023 by SI 2023/1283 (enabling regulations to be made). Carer's Leave Regulations 2024 (SI 2024/468) made 15 March 2024, in force 6 April 2024. Full entitlement operative from 6 April 2024.

secondReadingCommons.summary: Wendy Chamberlain moved the Second Reading with cross-party support. Shaun Bailey MP (Conservative) noted that unpaid carers saved the state £135 billion at the beginning of the COVID-19 pandemic. The Bill passed without division. Government confirmed it would support the Bill and work with the sponsor to bring it into force.

secondReadingLords.summary: Lord Fox moved the Second Reading in the Lords. Baroness Pitkeathley, former CEO of Carers UK, spoke powerfully about the economic cost of carers leaving employment. Lord Kamall (Minister) confirmed Government support. The Work and Pensions Select Committee's 2018 report on employment support for carers was cited extensively — it had found that lost productivity from carers unable to work cost the economy significantly.

regulationsDebate.summary: Kevin Hollinrake (Minister) presented the draft Carer's Leave Regulations 2024 alongside the Protection from Redundancy regulations. Justin Madders (Labour shadow, now Minister for Employment Rights) indicated he wanted carer's leave to be paid. The Minister confirmed 4.9 million adult informal carers in the UK, approximately half of whom hold down a job — an estimated 2.5 million working carers.

significantCommitteeAmendments.amendment: No substantive amendments — Bill passed Committee without change
significantCommitteeAmendments.summary: The Bill enjoyed complete cross-party support and passed through both Houses without substantive amendment. The only significant point of parliamentary discussion was whether the leave should be paid or unpaid — the Government opted for unpaid leave as the initial statutory minimum, with employers free to offer paid leave.
significantCommitteeAmendments.outcome: Enacted as introduced — Government confirmed willingness to review the paid/unpaid question in future

precedingDocuments.title: Work and Pensions Select Committee: Employment Support for Carers (May 2018)
precedingDocuments.significance: The Committee found that balancing care with paid employment caused many carers to give up work or reduce hours, costing the economy in lost productivity and tax revenues. Recommended a statutory right to carer's leave.

precedingDocuments.title: Carers UK State of Caring Survey 2024
precedingDocuments.significance: Found that 40% of working carers had given up work entirely, 44% had reduced hours, and 25% had moved to more junior roles. Average relative income gap of up to 45% for those providing the most hours of care.

precedingDocuments.title: Family Resources Survey 2021-22
precedingDocuments.significance: Identified 4.9 million adult informal carers in the UK — the statistical basis for the Government's estimate that approximately 2.5 million working carers would benefit from the Act.

precedingDocuments.title: COVID-19 pandemic impact on carers
precedingDocuments.significance: At the beginning of the pandemic, unpaid carers saved the state an estimated £135 billion (cited by Shaun Bailey MP at Second Reading). The pandemic exposed the vulnerability of informal care arrangements and increased political urgency for legislative reform.

leadingCases.name: No reported tribunal decisions under the Carer's Leave Act 2023 as at March 2026
leadingCases.citation: N/A — Act in force from 6 April 2024
leadingCases.principle: The Act has been in force for approximately one year as at March 2026. Given the three-month limitation period for complaints about postponement or prevention of leave (ERA s.80M), and the standard limitation for unfair dismissal complaints, the first tribunal decisions are anticipated from mid-2024 onwards. No reported decisions have been identified as at March 2026.
leadingCases.significance: SURVEILLANCE — Eileen must monitor BAILII and ET Register for the first decisions interpreting the scope of 'long-term care need', 'dependant', employer postponement powers, and the automatic unfair dismissal protection.

leadingCases.name: Qua v John Ford Morrison Solicitors
leadingCases.principle: Pre-Act case on the existing right to time off for dependants (ERA 1996 s.57A). Established that the right to time off for dependants covers only emergency or unexpected situations — not planned or foreseen caring commitments. This gap in protection was the direct catalyst for the Carer's Leave Act 2023, which covers planned and foreseen caring.
leadingCases.heldText: The right to time off for dependants under s.57A is limited to taking action which is 'necessary' because of an unexpected or sudden event. It does not extend to planned caring arrangements or long-term care needs.
leadingCases.significance: CRITICAL — demonstrates precisely why the 2023 Act was needed. The pre-existing s.57A right was limited to emergencies. An employee needing to take a parent to a routine hospital appointment, attend a planned care review, or provide scheduled respite care had no statutory right to leave. The 2023 Act fills this exact gap.

sections.title: Entitlement to carer's leave — Day 1 right to one week unpaid leave per year
sections.summary: Creates a new statutory right for EMPLOYEES (not workers — this is an employee-only right) to be absent from work on unpaid leave in order to provide or arrange care for a dependant with a long-term care need. The right is a Day 1 entitlement — no qualifying period of service. Employees are entitled to one week of carer's leave in any rolling 12-month period, regardless of how many dependants they have. The leave can be taken in half-days, full days, or as a continuous block of one week. The leave is UNPAID — the employer has no statutory obligation to pay, though may choose to do so.
sections.text:
The Secretary of State must make regulations entitling an employee to be absent from work on leave under this section in order to provide or arrange care for a dependant with a long-term care need.

Key provisions (as enacted via Carer's Leave Regulations 2024, SI 2024/468):

(1) An employee is entitled to carer's leave in order to provide or arrange care for a dependant who has a long-term care need.

(2) The entitlement is to a period of absence equal to the employee's usual working week in any rolling 12-month period.

(3) 'Dependant' means:
(a) a spouse or civil partner of the employee,
(b) a child of the employee,
(c) a parent of the employee,
(d) a person who lives in the same household as the employee (other than by reason of being the employee's tenant, lodger, boarder, or employee), or
(e) a person who reasonably relies on the employee to provide or arrange care.

(4) 'Long-term care need' means:
(a) an illness or injury (whether physical or mental) that requires, or is likely to require, care for more than three months,
(b) a disability as defined under the Equality Act 2010, or
(c) issues related to old age.

(5) The leave can be taken as:
(a) individual half-days,
(b) individual full days, or
(c) a continuous block of up to one week.

(6) The leave is unpaid.
sections.keyPrinciple: Every employee in the UK has a Day 1 right to one week of unpaid carer's leave per year. No qualifying period. No evidence requirement. The definition of 'dependant' is deliberately broad — it includes anyone who 'reasonably relies on the employee to provide or arrange care', capturing elderly neighbours, close friends, and extended family members.
refs: Carer's Leave Regulations 2024 (SI 2024/468) — the detailed regulations
ERA 1996 s.57A (time off for dependants — emergencies only, separate right)
ERA 1996 s.80I (parental leave — 18 weeks unpaid, separate right for parents)
Equality Act 2010 s.6 (definition of disability — referenced in 'long-term care need')
Qua v John Ford Morrison Solicitors [2003] ICR 482 (s.57A limited to emergencies — why the 2023 Act was needed)

regulatoryGuidance.title: Carer's leave guidance
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Comprehensive ACAS guidance covering entitlement, calculation of a week's leave for variable hours workers, notice requirements, postponement, and interaction with other leave types

regulatoryGuidance.title: Carer's leave — employer guidance
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government overview of the statutory right including entitlement, how to request, and employer obligations

regulatoryGuidance.title: The Carer's Leave Act 2023 — guidance for carers
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Worker-facing guidance from the leading carers' charity. Useful for Eileen when advising from the worker perspective.

commonErrors: Requiring a qualifying period of service before allowing carer's leave — it is a Day 1 right with no qualifying period
Limiting carer's leave to one dependant — the one-week entitlement applies regardless of how many dependants the employee has, and can be split between multiple dependants

Treating the right as applicable only to family members — the definition of 'dependant' includes anyone who 'reasonably relies on the employee to provide or arrange care', including elderly neighbours or close friends
Confusing carer's leave with time off for dependants (s.57A) — carer's leave is for PLANNED caring, s.57A is for EMERGENCIES. They are separate and additional rights
Assuming that old age alone is insufficient — 'issues related to old age' is a standalone category of long-term care need, separate from illness, injury, or disability
Failing to calculate leave correctly for variable-hours workers — the Regulations specify an averaging calculation over the preceding 52 weeks (or length of employment if less)

Extending carer's leave to 'workers' (limb b of ERA 1996 s.230(3)) — the right applies ONLY to employees, not to workers. This is a narrower scope than the Tips Act 2023 (which covers workers) and reflects a deliberate parliamentary choice. Agency workers, casual workers on zero-hours contracts, and other non-employee workers are NOT entitled to carer's leave

Limiting 'care' to hands-on personal care only — the Act covers both 'providing' care (direct personal care, meals, companionship) AND 'arranging' care (attending medical appointments to arrange treatment, organising a care package, meeting with social services, managing financial affairs related to care, accompanying a dependant to a benefits assessment). The ACAS guidance gives examples including providing meals and company for an elderly neighbour while their main carer is away

Assuming the employee must be the primary or sole carer — the Act does not require the employee to be the primary carer. An employee who provides supplementary or occasional care to a dependant with a long-term care need is entitled to carer's leave

sections.title: Notice requirements — how to request carer's leave
sections.summary: Employees must give notice before taking carer's leave. The notice period is either twice as many days as the period of leave requested, or three days, whichever is the greater. For example: half a day's leave requires three days' notice; two days' leave requires four days' notice; five days' leave requires ten days' notice. The notice does not have to be in writing. The employer may waive the notice requirement.
sections.text:
Regulation 4 of the Carer's Leave Regulations 2024:

(1) An employee who wishes to take carer's leave must give notice to their employer.

(2) The notice must be given at least—
(a) twice as many days before the day on which the period of leave is to begin as the number of days in the period, or
(b) three days before the day on which the period of leave is to begin,
whichever is the greater.

(3) The notice must specify—
(a) the dates on which the period of leave is to begin and end, and
(b) that the employee meets the conditions of entitlement.

(4) The notice need not be in writing.

(5) The employer may waive the notice requirement.
sections.keyPrinciple: The notice calculation is 'double the leave requested or three days, whichever is greater.' Employers cannot impose a more onerous notice requirement than the statutory formula. If an employee cannot give the minimum notice due to urgent caring needs, they should use time off for dependants (s.57A) instead.
refs: ERA 1996 s.57A (time off for dependants — no notice required for emergencies)
Carer's Leave Regulations 2024 Reg.5 (how leave is taken — half-days, full days, blocks)
ACAS guidance on carer's leave (notice calculation examples and tables)
commonErrors: Requiring written notice when the Regulations expressly state notice need not be in writing — a verbal request is sufficient
Imposing a longer notice period than the statutory formula — the employer cannot require more notice than twice the leave period or three days (whichever is greater)

Refusing leave because the employee gave less than the statutory notice — the employer should consider waiving the notice requirement or directing the employee to s.57A (time off for dependants) as an alternative

Failing to calculate leave correctly for employees with variable or irregular hours — Regulation 6 requires an averaging calculation: divide the total hours worked in the preceding 52 weeks (or the full period of employment if less than 52 weeks) by 52 (or the number of weeks employed). For an employee who has worked less than one complete week, a week equals the hours they would normally be expected to work in that week

sections.title: Evidence — employer CANNOT require evidence of caring responsibilities
sections.summary: The employer cannot require the employee to provide evidence of their caring responsibilities or the dependant's long-term care need as a condition of granting carer's leave. This is a deliberate policy choice: the employee self-certifies that they meet the conditions. The employer must take the request at face value.
sections.text:
Regulation 7 of the Carer's Leave Regulations 2024:

The employer may not require the employee to provide evidence of—
(a) the identity of the person for whom the employee provides or arranges care, or
(b) the nature of the long-term care need of that person,
as a condition of granting carer's leave.
sections.keyPrinciple: NO EVIDENCE REQUIRED. The employer cannot ask for medical evidence, GP letters, care plans, or proof of the dependant's condition. The employee self-certifies. This protects the privacy of both the employee and the dependant and prevents employers from creating barriers to accessing the right.
refs: Carer's Leave Regulations 2024 Reg.4(3)(b) (employee must state they meet the conditions — self-certification)
DPA 2018 / UK GDPR (data minimisation principle — employer should not collect more personal data than necessary)
ACAS guidance (confirms employers should not demand evidence but may use a self-certification form)
commonErrors: Requiring a GP letter or medical evidence before granting carer's leave — this is expressly prohibited by Regulation 7
Demanding the name of the person being cared for — the employer cannot require the identity of the dependant
Creating a policy that requires documentary evidence as a pre-condition — any such policy is unlawful and must be removed
Questioning the genuineness of the employee's caring responsibilities as a basis for refusing leave — the right is self-certified and the employer must accept the declaration

sections.title: Postponement of carer's leave — employer's limited right to delay
sections.summary: An employer CANNOT refuse carer's leave. However, the employer may postpone the requested period of leave if the employer reasonably considers that the operation of the business would be unduly disrupted if the employee were absent during the requested period. If postponing, the employer must agree an alternative date within one month of the original requested date and must put the reasons for postponement in writing within seven days of the request (or before the leave was due to start, whichever is earlier).
sections.text:
Regulation 8 of the Carer's Leave Regulations 2024:

(1) An employer may postpone a period of carer's leave where the employer reasonably considers that the operation of the business would be unduly disrupted if the employee took the leave during the period requested.

(2) Where an employer postpones a period of carer's leave, the employer must—
(a) allow the employee to take a period of leave of the same duration within the period of one month beginning with the day on which the postponed period of leave was due to begin, and
(b) give the employee notice in writing of the postponement and the reason for it within 7 days of the employee's notice, or before the leave was due to start, whichever is the earlier.

(3) The notice must specify the dates on which the employer proposes the employee should take the leave.
sections.keyPrinciple: The employer can DELAY carer's leave but CANNOT REFUSE it. The threshold is high: 'unduly disruptive' — not merely inconvenient. The employer must agree a new date within one month and must explain the reasons in writing within seven days.
refs: ERA 1996 s.80M (tribunal complaint for unreasonable postponement or prevention of carer's leave)
ERA 1996 s.80I(5) (parental leave postponement — similar but not identical model)
ACAS guidance on carer's leave postponement
commonErrors: Refusing carer's leave outright — the employer has NO power to refuse, only to postpone. An outright refusal is a breach enforceable at tribunal
Postponing leave without agreeing a new date within one month — the one-month window is mandatory
Failing to give written reasons for postponement within seven days — this is a standalone obligation and failure constitutes a breach
Using 'busy period' as a routine justification for postponement — the threshold is 'unduly disruptive', which requires more than ordinary business inconvenience
Repeatedly postponing the same employee's carer's leave — a pattern of postponement could constitute prevention of leave, which is a separate and more serious breach

Failing to accommodate an employee's cancellation of carer's leave — if an employee needs to cancel a booked period of carer's leave, the employer should accept the cancellation. The unused leave remains available within the 12-month entitlement period. Employers should encourage employees to give as much notice as reasonably possible when cancelling

parts.title: Part 3 — Rights During Leave and Return to Work

sections.title: Rights during carer's leave — terms and conditions continue
sections.summary: During carer's leave, the employee is entitled to the benefit of all terms and conditions of employment that would have applied but for the absence — except remuneration. The employee continues to accrue annual leave, remains covered by any contractual benefits (staff discounts, pension contributions if applicable, private medical insurance), and remains bound by obligations of good faith, confidentiality, and notice requirements. On return, the employee is entitled to return to the same job.
sections.text:
Carer's Leave Regulations 2024:

During a period of carer's leave—
(a) the employee is entitled to the benefit of the terms and conditions of employment which would have applied if the employee had not been absent, and
(b) the employee is bound by any obligations arising under those terms and conditions (except in so far as they are inconsistent with the right to be absent).

An employee who returns to work after a period of carer's leave is entitled to return to the job in which the employee was employed before the absence.
sections.keyPrinciple: Carer's leave is a right to be absent WITH continuing employment terms — it is not a resignation or a break in service. Annual leave continues to accrue. Contractual benefits continue. The employee returns to the same job.
refs: ERA 1996 s.80K (terms and conditions during carer's leave)
ERA 1996 s.80L (right to return)
Working Time Regulations 1998 Reg.13 (annual leave continues to accrue during statutory leave)
ERA 1996 s.86 (notice rights — unaffected by carer's leave)
commonErrors: Suspending contractual benefits during carer's leave — all terms and conditions continue except pay
Failing to accrue annual leave during carer's leave — holiday entitlement continues to accrue
Offering a different job on return — the employee is entitled to return to the SAME job, not a suitable alternative
Treating the period of carer's leave as a break in continuous service — it is not

sections.title: Tribunal complaints — unreasonable postponement or prevention of carer's leave
sections.summary: An employee may present a complaint to an employment tribunal that the employer has unreasonably postponed a period of carer's leave or has prevented or attempted to prevent the employee from taking carer's leave. The limitation period is three months from the date of the matter complained of. The tribunal can make a declaration and award compensation.
sections.text:
80M Complaints to an employment tribunal

(1) An employee may complain to an employment tribunal that their employer—
(a) has unreasonably postponed a period of carer's leave, or
(b) has prevented or attempted to prevent the employee from taking carer's leave.

(2) The tribunal must not consider the complaint unless it is made—
(a) before the end of the period of three months beginning with the date (or last date) of the matter complained of, or
(b) within such further period as the tribunal considers reasonable in a case where it is satisfied that it was not reasonably practicable for the complaint to be made before the end of that period of three months.
sections.keyPrinciple: Two separate grounds for complaint: unreasonable postponement AND prevention/attempted prevention of leave. The three-month limitation period runs from the date of the employer's action (or last action in a course of conduct).
refs: ERA 1996 s.80M (complaint route)
ERA 1996 s.207B (ACAS early conciliation time extension applies)
Carer's Leave Regulations 2024 Reg.8 (postponement conditions — the standard against which 'unreasonable' is assessed)
commonErrors: Assuming there is no enforcement mechanism — the tribunal complaint route is clear and provides for both declaration and compensation
Failing to recognise that attempted prevention is a separate ground — even an unsuccessful attempt to prevent carer's leave is actionable

sections.num: s.2 + Schedule (ERA s.47C and s.99 amendments)
sections.title: Protection from detriment and automatic unfair dismissal for carer's leave
sections.summary: Two layers of worker protection. First, ERA 1996 s.47C (as amended by the Schedule) protects employees from being subjected to any detriment by the employer on the ground that the employee took or sought to take carer's leave, or that the employer believed the employee was likely to take carer's leave. Second, ERA 1996 s.99 (as amended) makes it automatically unfair to dismiss an employee for a reason connected with carer's leave — including taking leave, seeking to take leave, or the employer believing the employee intended to take leave. No qualifying period is required for automatic unfair dismissal claims connected to carer's leave.
sections.text:
Schedule to the Carer's Leave Act 2023 — Consequential Amendments:

Amendment to ERA 1996 s.47C (right not to suffer detriment):
An employee has the right not to be subjected to any detriment by any act, or deliberate failure to act, by the employer done on the ground that the employee—
(a) took, sought to take, or made use of the benefits of carer's leave, or
(b) was believed by the employer to be likely to take carer's leave.

Amendment to ERA 1996 s.99 (unfair dismissal — leave for family and domestic reasons):
An employee who is dismissed shall be regarded as unfairly dismissed if the reason or principal reason for the dismissal is connected with the employee taking, seeking to take, or making use of the benefits of carer's leave.

ERA 1996 s.108(3) is amended to add carer's leave to the list of reasons for which no qualifying period is required for an unfair dismissal complaint.
sections.keyPrinciple: Dismissal for taking or requesting carer's leave is AUTOMATICALLY UNFAIR — no two-year qualifying period required, and the basic award is not capped. Detriment protection extends to situations where the employer merely BELIEVES the employee is likely to take carer's leave — preventing employers from taking pre-emptive action against employees known to have caring responsibilities.
refs: ERA 1996 s.47C (detriment for family leave reasons — carer's leave now added)
ERA 1996 s.99 (automatic unfair dismissal for family leave reasons — carer's leave now added)
ERA 1996 s.108(3) (qualifying period exclusions — carer's leave now listed)
ERA 1996 s.104 (assertion of statutory right — additional protection route)
Equality Act 2010 s.13/s.15 (direct disability discrimination / discrimination arising from disability — overlapping claims where the dependant's disability triggers the caring need)
commonErrors: Dismissing an employee who has taken carer's leave — this is automatically unfair dismissal with no qualifying period and no cap on the basic award
Subjecting an employee to detriment (reduced hours, shift changes, exclusion from promotion) because they took or requested carer's leave — this is unlawful detriment

Failing to recognise the BELIEF ground — even if the employee has not actually taken carer's leave, treating them less favourably because the employer believes they are likely to take it is unlawful detriment

Overlooking the Equality Act overlap — where the caring need arises from the dependant's disability, there may be additional claims for associative disability discrimination (Coleman v Attridge Law [2008] CJEU C-303/06)

parts.title: Part 5 — Interaction with Other Leave Rights and Future Development

sections.num: Cross-reference: interaction with other leave types
sections.title: Carer's leave in the context of other statutory and contractual leave entitlements
sections.summary: Carer's leave is a SEPARATE and ADDITIONAL right — it does not replace any existing leave entitlement. An employee with caring responsibilities may also be entitled to: time off for dependants (s.57A ERA 1996 — emergencies), parental leave (s.76 ERA 1996 — up to 18 weeks per child), annual leave (WTR 1998), flexible working (ERA 1996 s.80F), and contractual compassionate or carers' leave. Where an employer already offers paid carer's leave in excess of the statutory minimum, the statutory entitlement does not create an additional week — but if the contractual entitlement is less than a week, the employee can 'top up' with statutory carer's leave to reach the one-week minimum.
sections.text:
Interaction map:

(1) Time off for dependants (ERA 1996 s.57A): SEPARATE right. Covers emergencies and unexpected situations only. Carer's leave covers planned and foreseen caring. An employee whose parent has a sudden fall would use s.57A; the same employee taking their parent to a planned hospital appointment the following week would use carer's leave.

(2) Parental leave (ERA 1996 s.76): SEPARATE right. Up to 18 weeks unpaid per child, for the purpose of caring for a child under 18. A parent can use both parental leave AND carer's leave for the same child if the child has a long-term care need.

(3) Annual leave (WTR 1998 Reg.13): SEPARATE right. The employee should not be required to use annual leave in place of carer's leave.

(4) Flexible working (ERA 1996 s.80F): COMPLEMENTARY right. An employee with ongoing caring responsibilities may benefit from both carer's leave (for specific care episodes) and a flexible working arrangement (for ongoing pattern adjustment).

(5) Contractual carers'/compassionate leave: Where the employer offers paid leave that covers planned caring, this may satisfy the statutory obligation. If it falls short of one week, the employee can top up with unpaid statutory carer's leave.
sections.keyPrinciple: Carer's leave is ADDITIONAL to all other leave rights. Employers should not require employees to exhaust annual leave or use time off for dependants before accessing carer's leave. Each right has its own scope and conditions.
refs: ERA 1996 s.57A (time off for dependants — emergencies only)
ERA 1996 s.76 (parental leave — 18 weeks per child)
WTR 1998 Reg.13 (annual leave — 5.6 weeks per year)
ERA 1996 s.80F (flexible working — right to request from Day 1)
Employment Rights Act 2025 (potential for paid carer's leave — surveillance pending)

leadingCases.name: Coleman v Attridge Law
leadingCases.court: Court of Justice of the European Union
leadingCases.principle: Associative discrimination — an employee who suffers less favourable treatment because of a dependant's disability can bring a direct disability discrimination claim, even though the employee themselves is not disabled.
leadingCases.heldText: The Equal Treatment Directive prohibits direct discrimination on grounds of disability against an employee who is not herself disabled but who is treated less favourably because she is the primary carer of a disabled child.
leadingCases.significance: CRITICAL for carer's leave intersection — an employer who treats an employee unfavourably because their dependant is disabled may face BOTH a carer's leave detriment claim AND an associative disability discrimination claim under the Equality Act 2010.

commonErrors: Requiring employees to use annual leave before carer's leave — carer's leave is a separate and additional entitlement
Directing employees to use time off for dependants (s.57A) when the caring need is planned — s.57A is for emergencies only, carer's leave is for planned caring
Failing to recognise the associative discrimination overlap — where caring is connected to a dependant's disability, the Equality Act 2010 provides additional protections beyond the Carer's Leave Act

pendingAmendments.instrument: Employment Rights Act 2025 — potential for paid carer's leave
pendingAmendments.status: The ERA 2025 may introduce paid carer's leave. Justin Madders MP (Minister for Employment Rights) stated during the Carer's Leave Regulations debate (February 2024) that he wanted carer's leave to be paid. The Liberal Democrats have formally called for paid carer's leave as policy. Carers UK anniversary briefing (April 2025) identified paid leave as the next priority. Monitor secondary legislation and commencement orders.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — if carer's leave becomes paid, employer cost obligations change significantly

pendingAmendments.status: The Act has been in force since 6 April 2024. The three-month limitation period for postponement/prevention complaints means the first tribunal decisions could appear from mid-2024. Key areas for judicial development: meaning of 'unduly disruptive' for postponement, scope of 'reasonably relies on the employee for care' in the dependant definition, and interaction with disability discrimination claims.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — first decisions will establish interpretive baselines for 'unduly disruptive' and the broad dependant definition

pendingAmendments.instrument: Carers UK State of Caring Survey — annual monitoring
pendingAmendments.status: Carers UK publishes annual survey data on the impact of caring on employment. The 2024 survey found 40% of carers had given up work and 44% had reduced hours. Monitoring whether the 2025 and 2026 surveys show improvement following the Act's commencement.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: LOW for individual compliance but HIGH for understanding the policy context and whether further legislative intervention is anticipated
